nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers, streams, and flooded areas with dense aquatic vegetation, submerged roots, and leaf litter; often found near riverbanks and in backwaters with soft, slightly acidic to neutral water

decorPreferences: PVC tubes or bamboo tubes as daytime hiding retreats
dense planting (Amazon swords, vallisneria, java fern) along sides and back
driftwood and branchy wood for shelter
soft substrate (fine sand) with leaf litter
dim or subdued lighting with floating plants to diffuse light

diet.notes: Insectivore/carnivore in the wild and captivity. Accepts live and frozen bloodworms, blackworms, white worms, tubifex, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Many individuals can be trained to accept high-quality meaty frozen foods and sinking carnivore pellets over time. Nocturnal feeder — best fed after lights out. Small worms (blackworms, bloodworms) are typically the most readily accepted food; live foods are strongly preferred by newly imported specimens. Avoid plant-based foods.

goodWith: medium-sized peaceful tetras (e.g., black skirt, bleeding heart)
angelfish (with caution — monitor for fin nipping)
other Glass Knifefish of similar size (kept in odd-number groups)
avoidWith: other weakly electric fish from different genera (electric field interference)
Black Ghost Knifefish (Apteronotus albifrons — territorial and much larger)
very small fish or fry (predation risk)
species requiring salt medication (salt disrupts electric organ discharge)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image of Eigenmannia virescens showing characteristic transparent/greenish body and elongated knifefish form.

summary: The Glass Knifefish is a South American weakly electric fish prized for its near-transparent body through which internal organs are visible. Unlike the larger Black Ghost Knifefish, it schools in nature and tolerates conspecifics well. Produces a continuous weak electric field used for navigation and communication — not harmful to people or fish, but salt medications are contraindicated.
careNotes:
The Glass Knifefish (Eigenmannia virescens) is one of the most beginner-accessible knifefish, though it still warrants intermediate classification due to its size (~40 cm adults) and specific requirements. It belongs to the family Sternopygidae — distinct from the more commonly kept Black Ghost Knifefish (Apteronotus albifrons, family Apteronotidae), which is larger, jet-black, and more territorial.

This species generates a continuous weak electric organ discharge (EOD) used for electrolocation and communication with conspecifics — a passive sense, not a shock. The electric field is disrupted by salt and certain medications; salt-based treatments are therefore contraindicated. Copper-based medications should also be avoided as the fish is scaleless and copper-sensitive.

Unusually among knifefish, Eigenmannia virescens is a schooling species in the wild and keeps well in groups of 3 or more (odd numbers are often cited to reduce pairing aggression). Groups of the same species from the same family can be kept together; mixing with other electric species such as Black Ghosts risks electric field interference and territorial conflict.

A minimum tank of 200 L with at least 120 cm length is advised for a small group; adults approach 40–45 cm. Provide ample hiding spots — PVC tubes, bamboo segments, or dense planting — as this is a strongly nocturnal species that will spend daylight hours concealed. Feed after lights out; bloodworms, blackworms, and frozen meaty foods are accepted. Live foods ease the transition for new specimens.

Water should be soft to moderately hard, slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), warm (23–28°C), and well-oxygenated with gentle flow. The species is sensitive to deteriorating water quality. Weekly partial water changes of 25–30% are important. A tight-fitting lid is essential — like most knifefish, Eigenmannia can jump and is a moderate escape risk.
breedingNotes: Rarely bred in home aquaria. In the wild, Eigenmannia virescens undergoes seasonal spawning tied to flooding cycles. Egg-scatterers that spawn among dense vegetation. Reports of captive spawning exist but are uncommon; conditioning with live foods, a large tank, and simulated seasonal cues (temperature drop, increased water changes mimicking rainy season) have been cited in specialist accounts. Sexing is difficult; females are generally more robust. No parental care; eggs and fry are at risk of predation by adults. Practical home breeding is not realistically achievable without dedicated effort and large species-only setups.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Eigenmannia virescens
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range (23–28°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness, native range (widespread South America), nocturnal behavior, electric organ details, schooling nature, diet (insectivore/worms), breeding notes, and compatibility guidance including contraindication of salt.

additional.name: FishBase — Eigenmannia virescens
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 45 cm TL), taxonomy (Sternopygidae, Gymnotiformes), native range (La Plata, Amazon, Orinoco, and Magdalena basins), and temperature range. Confirmed distribution across Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Peru, Ecuador, Bolivia, Argentina, Paraguay, Uruguay.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Knifefish Care
additional.notes: Supplementary trade care guide. Confirmed intermediate care level, nocturnal behavior, diet of worms and frozen meaty foods, hiding-spot requirements, and avoidance of salt medications for electric fish. Tank size and schooling behavior cross-checked.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Eigenmannia virescens
additional.notes: Confirmed electric organ discharge (EOD) biology, transparent body description, schooling disposition, and distinction from Apteronotus albifrons (Black Ghost). Noted salt contraindication due to EOD disruption and copper sensitivity as a scaleless species.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Gymnotiformes overview
additional.notes: Supplementary source on weakly electric fish husbandry. Confirmed family Sternopygidae distinction from Apteronotidae, continuous wave-type EOD, schooling behavior in the wild, and nocturnal feeding habits. Also noted breeding as rarely accomplished in captivity.

fish.breedingNotes: Rarely bred in captivity. Seasonal spawners triggered by flooding-season cues; egg-scatterers with no parental care. Conditioning with live foods and simulated rainy-season water changes (cooler, softer water) has been cited in specialist accounts. Sexing difficult; females are deeper-bodied.
